value:
Recently, I led a CEO search for a financial services firm and presented the offer to a strong candidate. He had successfully led a similar company and was highly recommended by two board members. He accepted with enthusiasm, then called a few days later to decline. What happened?
It turns out he considered the weight of what was being asked: aggressive growth targets with significant compensation tied to delivery, and he wasn’t sure he could deliver. The hiring committee felt he had choked. I think he made the right call.
What stood out wasn’t that a candidate turned down a CEO role. That happens. It was how late the doubt surfaced, and how rarely senior leaders allow themselves to surface it at all.
More often, the opposite happens. Leaders say yes. They take the role. And only months in, when the context proves more complex than expected, do the cracks begin to show.
The Confidence Gap No One Talks About
At the CEO and C-suite level, confidence is table stakes. You don’t get there without it. But in today’s environment, confidence has become a double-edged sword.
The business landscape is shifting faster than most leadership playbooks can keep up with. AI is reshaping decision-making. The economy is more volatile. Organizations are flatter, leaner, and under constant pressure to do more with less.
In that context, past success is an increasingly unreliable predictor of future performance. The data on CEO turnover bears this out. According to Russell Reynolds Associates’ Global CEO Turnover Index, 234 CEOs departed their roles globally in 2025, a 16 percent increase year-over-year and the second consecutive record year. Eleven of those CEO appointments in 2025 lasted less than a year.
And yet, boards and hiring committees continue to overweight experience. They look for leaders who have “done it before,” ideally in a similar company, role, and mandate. On paper, it feels like de-risking the hire.
In reality, it can create a different kind of risk: overconfidence rooted in outdated experience.
When Experience Becomes a Trap
When leaders succeed in one context, they build a mental model of what works. That model becomes their default operating system.
The problem is that today’s challenges often don’t map cleanly to past experiences. What worked before may be directionally useful, but it’s rarely sufficient.
I’ve seen this play out repeatedly. A highly accomplished executive steps into a new role and immediately begins applying the strategies that made them successful in their last position. At first, it creates momentum. But over time, misalignment emerges. The organization resists. Results stall.
It’s not a capability issue. It’s a context issue.
The candidate who turned down the CEO role recognized, perhaps intuitively, that the gap between his past experience and the demands of this role was wider than it appeared. He chose not to rely on confidence alone to bridge that gap.
His perspective is one we should probably see more often.
What Hiring Decision Makers Miss
Boards are under pressure to deliver results, and hiring a CEO is one of the highest-stakes decisions they make. It’s understandable that they gravitate toward proven leaders.
But “proven” needs to be redefined.
Instead of asking, “Has this person done the job before?” boards should be asking:
- How does this leader learn in real time?
- Are they asking the right questions?
- Do they have the humility to question their own assumptions?
- Can they build a team that compensates for what they don’t know?
These are the capabilities that actually correlate with performance now. Korn Ferry research found that companies with the highest concentration of learning-agile executives produce 25 percent higher profit margins than their peers. Adaptability is not a soft attribute. It is a measurable performance lever.
And the gap is not abstract. DDI’s Global Leadership Forecast 2025 found that 61 percent of leaders identify managing change as a critical capability, but only 36 percent have been developed in this area. Even the leaders who have done it before are not necessarily ready for what is being asked of them now.
The best leaders I work with are those who are acutely aware of what they don’t know and actively close that gap.
The Discipline of Saying No
There’s another lesson here, particularly for senior executives navigating their own careers.
At a certain level, opportunities become more attractive on paper and more complex in reality. Higher compensation can be hard to turn down, but it will likely come largely in the form of incentive bonuses and stock options tied to delivery.
The margin for error is narrowing. Saying yes to the wrong role at this stage is both a career risk and a reputational one.
The candidate in this search did something many executives struggle to do: he paused long enough to assess not just whether he wanted the role, but whether he could truly deliver in it. That requires a level of honesty that can feel uncomfortable, especially when external validation is high.
Being qualified is not the same as being ready.
A Different Standard for Leadership
If there is one shift I would advocate for, both for boards and for executives, it is this: we need to move from a model of leadership based on certainty to one based on dynamism and context.
Dynamic leaders are reflective and adaptive. They recalibrate when needed. They are willing to walk away from opportunities that don’t align with their ability to deliver.
The candidate who said no didn’t lack confidence. He had something rarer. He had the judgment to know when his confidence wasn’t enough.
That is the standard we should be hiring for.
